coredata.dc:title: N,P co-doped carbon quantum dots bridge g-C3N4 and SnO2: Accelerating charge transport in S-scheme heterojunction for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Shang, Yaru
Liu, Tianxing
Chen, Gang
Alborzi, Ehsan
Yong, Xue
Wang, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of photocatalysts with efficient electron-hole separation efficiency is the key to achieving sustainable utilization of solar energy. Herein, a three-phase composite of N,P co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs), SnO2 nanowires, and g-C3N4 nanotubes (CNNT) is synthesized, forming an S-scheme SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction. The construction of S-scheme heterojunction not only improves the reduction ability due to band bending, but also promotes the separation of carriers. The conductive NPCDs are located between SnO2 and CNNT and formed as a unique charge transfer channel that accelerates carriers transfer between S-scheme heterojunction interfaces. Additionally, NPCDs serves as electron acceptors to receive electrons from the conduction band of CNNT, thereby further facilitating the carrier separation. This synergistically boosts the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT three-phase catalysts, leading to a high H2 yield (10.73 mmol·g−1·h−1) and outstanding cyclic stability. This study offers a strategy for designing heterostructure photocatalysts that enhanced charge separation through interface engineering.
coredata.dcterms:subject: G-C3N4 nanotubes
SnO2 nanowires
Carbon quantum dots
Charge transport channel
S-scheme
Photocatalytic H2 production

This work was financially supported by project s of Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province , China ( LH2021B009 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of synthesis for SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT. SEM images of (b) CNNT and (c) NPCDs/CNNT. (d) TEM and (e-i) HRTEM images of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT. (j-n) TEM and the corresponding elemental mapping images of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT.
(a) XRD patterns. (b) FTIR spectra. (c) The up-converted PL spectra of NPCDs.
XPS spectra of samples. (a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s, (c) Sn 3d, (d) O 1 s.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra. (b) Tauc plots. (c) XPS valence band spectra. (d) Schematic diagram of band structures.

(a) PL spectra. (b) Photocurrent response curves. (c) EIS curves (the equivalent circuit impedance model inset). (d) LSV curves. (e) The relationship between current density and scan rate. (f,g) Photocatalytic H2 production performance of different samples. (h) Cyclic tests. (i) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT and corresponding AQE.

Calculated work functions of (a) CNNT, (b) NPCDs, and (c) SnO2. (d) Schematic diagram of band structure configuration in SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT. (e) The side view of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT model. (f) The average electron density difference (Δ ρ) along Z-direction for SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT. (g) The side view of charge density difference.
(a) ESR spectra of (a) DMPO−·OH and (b) DMPO−·O2 −. (c) Schematic diagram of the photocatalytic reaction.

head.ce:title: N,P co-doped carbon quantum dots bridge g-C3N4 and SnO2: Accelerating charge transport in S-scheme heterojunction for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MIIT Key Laboratory of Critical Materials Technology for New Energy Conversion and Storage, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, 150001, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The University of Sheffield, Brook Hill, Sheffield S3 7HF, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, The University of Sheffield

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The University of Sheffield, Brook Hill Sheffield, S3 7HF, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, The University of Sheffield, S3 7HF, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, The University of Sheffield

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, The University of Sheffield, S3 7HF, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of photocatalysts with efficient electron-hole separation efficiency is the key to achieving sustainable utilization of solar energy. Herein, a three-phase composite of N,P co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs), SnO2 nanowires, and g-C3N4 nanotubes (CNNT) is synthesized, forming an S-scheme SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction. The construction of S-scheme heterojunction not only improves the reduction ability due to band bending, but also promotes the separation of carriers. The conductive NPCDs are located between SnO2 and CNNT and formed as a unique charge transfer channel that accelerates carriers transfer between S-scheme heterojunction interfaces. Additionally, NPCDs serves as electron acceptors to receive electrons from the conduction band of CNNT, thereby further facilitating the carrier separation. This synergistically boosts the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT three-phase catalysts, leading to a high H2 yield (10.73 mmol·g−1·h−1) and outstanding cyclic stability. This study offers a strategy for designing heterostructure photocatalysts that enhanced charge separation through interface engineering.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) plays a critical role in low-carbon economy as a clean, sustainable and promising secondary energy [1,2]. Industrial methods for hydrogen production, including water electrolysis, steam reforming, coal gasification methods, etc., will cause a lot of energy loss and environmental pollution [3,4]. Photocatalytic water splitting offers a green and sustainable way to produce H2 using abundant Earth−water and sunlight [5,6]. Metal-free graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted a lot of attention in the field of photocatalytic H2 evolution because of its non-toxic, low cost, easy preparation, visible light response and chemical stability [7,8]. However, carrier separation and transport restrictions are still one of the most challenging issues affecting the photocatalytic performance. As for g-C3N4, the construction of ultra-thin or hollow nanostructures is an effective modification method to promote carrier separation and increase active sites, such as microspheres, nanosheets, and nanotubes, etc., rather than directly obtaining bulk g-C3N4 through thermal condensation [9,10]. For example, Fu et al. synthesized a porous few-layer C3N4, which has a H2 production rate of 7990 μmol·g−1·h−1, 26 times that of bulk C3N4 [11]. Wu et al. prepared hierarchical mesoporous polymeric C3N4 microspheres using SiO2 microspheres as a template [12], exhibiting enhanced H2 evolution activity (4635 μmol·g−1·h−1). Thereinto, the thin-wall hollow structure not only facilitates the rapid transfer of carriers to the surface, but also boosts light utilization due to the scattering and reflection of light in the cavity [13,14].

The construction of heterojunction is another effective strategy to improve the charge separation efficiency. In recent years, the establishment of semiconductor/g-C3N4 heterojunctions to enhance photocatalytic performance has been widely studied, such as W18O49/g-C3N4 [15], CdS/g-C3N4 [16], SnO2/g-C3N4 [17], ZnIn2S4/g-C3N4 [18], etc. Among them, n-type semiconductor SnO2 has a wide band gap (∼3.5 eV) [19], resulting in no response to visible light. Furthermore, its conduction band (CB) position is usually more positive than reduction potential of H+/H2 (0 V vs NHE), so it is rarely used as a photocatalytic H2 production material. Currently, the band structure of SnO2 can be adjusted through some modification methods to ensure that it has a more negative CB position. The band structure of the type-I heterojunction is in the form of straddling gap [20], where electrons and holes are aggregated in the CB and valence band (VB) of one semiconductor, which is not conducive to carrier separation. Some SnO2/g-C3N4 heterojunction photocatalysts have been successfully prepared [21,22], most of which constitute type-II heterojunction, which can favor carrier separation but reduce the redox properties of the catalysts. In stark contrast to type-II heterojunction, the charge migration path of S-scheme heterojunction facilitates carrier separation while preserving holes with strong oxidation and electrons with strong reduction properties [23,24], realizing a potential band structure for photocatalytic H2 production. Therefore, the construction of S-scheme SnO2/g-C3N4 heterojunction is an ideal method to ameliorate the photocatalytic H2 production property which can ignore the shortcomings of failed H2 evolution caused by the positive CB of SnO2. In addition, anchoring conductive solid media as channels in S-scheme heterojunction can further accelerate the migration of carriers between heterojunction interfaces [17,25,26]. As a zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have excellent electrical conductivity and up-conversion optical properties [27], which can not only serve as electron bridging channels, but also boost the utilization of light, contributing to enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The introduction of heteroatoms (N, P) in CDs can adjust the charge redistribution of C atoms, which can generate new energy levels and further optimize the electron transfer and light utilization ability of CDs. [25]. Some materials containing SnO2, CDs or g-C3N4 have been reported in the literature, but their three-phase composite materials are currently less studied, and the mechanism of action among them is not clear.

Herein, g-C3N4 nanotubes (CNNT) were prepared by supramolecular self-assembly, and then N and P co-doped carbon dots (NPCDs) and SnO2 nanowires were modified to synthesize S-scheme SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction composites by a two-step method. On the one hand, the construction of S-scheme heterojunction promotes carrier separation, and on the other hand, it also preserves the strongly reducing electrons in CNNT. Simultaneously, NPCDs act as charge bridging channels at the heterojunction interface to further expedite the separation of carriers. The photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction hybrid was significantly advanced, up to 12.3 times that of bulk g-C3N4. Through many photoelectrochemical analysis, DFT calculation, and photocatalytic performance comparison, the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance was proposed.

Details of the experimental section are supplemented in the supporting information. The porous g-C3N4 nanotubes were prepared by hydrothermal method. During hydrothermal process, unique nanostructures can be easily and controllably customized by supramolecular self-assembly. Subsequently, the NPCDs and SnO2 nanowires were recombined on the CNNT by ultrasonic methods, which could ensure their structural stability and uniform dispersion on the CNNT.

Fig. 1a manifested the synthesis process diagram of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT hybrid. First, we crafted porous g-C3N4 nanotubes (CNNT) with a diameter of ∼500 nm by hydrothermal self-assembly method (Fig. 1b). This porous nanotube structure greatly shortens the migration distance of charge. The anchoring process of NPCDs on CNNT had no influence on the morphology of nanotubes (Fig. 1c). From the TEM images of NPCDs/CNNT (Fig. S1), the NPCDs were about 5–10 nm in diameter and uniformly dispersed on the surface of the CNNT. As-prepared SnO2 exhibited the nanowire morphology with 70–100 nm in length and ∼5 nm in diameter (Fig. S2a). The lattice fringe of 0.33 nm represented the (110) crystal plane of SnO2 (Fig. S2b) [28]. After further loading of SnO2 nanowires on NPCDs/CNNT, it was clear that SnO2 nanowires (∼5 nm) were evenly distributed on the surface (Fig. 1d and its inset). In the HRTEM images of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT (Fig. 1e,f), the (110) crystal plane (lattice fringes of 0.33 nm) of SnO2 and the (100) crystal plane (lattice fringes of 0.25 nm) of NPCDs could be seen [29], further verifying the successful embellishment of NPCDs and SnO2. To uncover the load position and interface information of NPCDs and SnO2 on CNNT in-depth, higher magnification HRTEM images were utilized (Fig. 1g-i). The typical hexagonal graphitic crystal structure representing NPCDs was observed in Fig. 1g. Surprisingly, NPCDs with hexagonal graphitic structure were precisely covered with SnO2 nanowires (Fig. 1h), that is, NPCDs bridging between CNNT and SnO2. Besides, the direct contact interface between CNNT and SnO2 has also been found owing to the small size and low content of NPCDs (Fig. 1i). Elemental mapping images of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT demonstrated that Sn and O elements were uniformly distributed on the surface (Fig. 1j-n). The BET tests confirmed that the construction of nanotubes and the decoration of SnO2 and NPCDs increased the specific surface area of the catalyst (Fig. S3).

To capture structural and compositional information, XRD patterns and FTIR spectra were performed. As displayed in Fig. 2a, the SnO2 nanowires matched to the tetragonal phase SnO2 (JCPDS NO.41–1445) [30], and the peaks at 26.6°, 33.9°, 51.8°, and 57.8° belong to the (110), (101), (211), and (002) crystal planes, respectively. The two diffraction peaks of 12.9° and 27.8° in Bulk-CN, CNNT, NPCDs/CNNT, and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNTs were originated from the stacking of in-plane repeating units and interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic structures [31]. Apart from the diffraction peaks of g-C3N4, the peaks of SnO2 were also presented in SnO2/NPCDs/CNNTs. In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b), the peaks at 3050–3350 cm−1, 1145–1670 cm−1 and 810 cm−1 were assignable to the vibration modes of -OH/-NH, CN heterocycles and triazine unit, respectively [32]. Note that Sn-O bonds (590–760 cm−1) exist in the FTIR spectra of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT [33]. In addition, there are abundant functional groups in NPCDs, among which the peaks of 556, 953, 1085, 1290, 1461, 2865, and 3010–3300 cm−1 correspond to PO4 3−, P-OH, P = O, N-H, CC, C-H, and O-H/N-H chemical bonds, respectively [34,35]. In Fig. S4, the zeta potential implied that NPCDs were decorated on CNNT through electrostatic adsorption. The up-conversion PL spectra of NPCDs elucidated that NPCDs could absorb light at 600–900 nm and perform photoluminescence at a shorter wavelength (∼ 460 nm) (Fig. 2c) [36], which is beneficial to enhance the light utilization of catalyst.

The chemical states of catalysts were further confirmed by XPS spectra ( Fig. 3). For the C 1 s XPS spectra, the peaks of 284.8, 286.2, and 288.4 eV in NPCDs were assigned to the CC, C-O/C-N and CO bonds (Fig. 3a), respectively [35], while peaks at about 284.8, 286.1, and 288.1 eV in CNNT, NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT represented CC, C-NHx, and N-CN, respectively (Fig. 3a) [37]. For CNNT, NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT, the peaks in the N 1 s spectra were ascribed to C-NHx (401.4 eV), N-C3 (400.4 eV), and C-N = C (398.7 eV) (Fig. 3b) [38]. By contrast, the peaks of N 1 s and C 1 s in SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT shifted toward higher binding energy than those in NPCDs/CNNT, implying that there is an interaction between SnO2 and NPCDs/CNNT, and electrons tend to transfer from NPCDs/CNNT to SnO2 [39]. For the N 1 s spectra of NPCDs, the peak at 401.2 eV was ascribed to the C-N/N-P bond, while the peak at 399.1 eV was derived from the N-H bond, indicating the presence of active groups such as amino groups, consistent with previous FTIR analysis results. The P-O group (133.9 eV) and P-C/P-N peak (133.0 eV) also appeared in P 2p XPS spectra of NPCDs (Fig. S5) [40]. The above evidences that P and N elements are introduced into NPCDs, and their surface contains a large number of functional groups, boosting the electron transfer ability of carbon quantum dots. The area ratio of the C atoms in different chemical states and the percentage of element content in different samples were provided in Table S1 and Table S2. As depicted in Table S1 and S2, the CC area ratio and C/N ratio in NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT are higher than those in CNNT, suggesting the successful loading of NPCDs. In Sn 3d XPS spectra of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT (Fig. 3c), the peaks of Sn 3d5/2 (486.9 eV) and Sn 3d3/2 (495.5 eV) belong to Sn4+ [41]. From the observation in O 1 s spectra (Fig. 3d), there is a peak of Sn-O lattice oxygen (530.9 eV) in SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT [42]. The above analysis further validated the successful assembly of NPCDs and SnO2 on CNNTs.

UV–visible DRS spectra were carried out to elucidate the light absorption properties of the catalysts ( Fig. 4a). In the visible region, CNNT, NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNTs exhibited stronger light absorption than Bulk-CN. The Tauc plots evaluated that the band gaps of CNNT, NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2 were 2.69, 2.64 and 2.84 eV (Fig. 4b), respectively, while their valance bands were estimated to be approximately 1.87, 1.95 and 3.20 eV through XPS valence band spectra (Fig. 4c), respectively, thus determining the schematic diagram of the band structure (Fig. 4d).

The influences of NPCDs and SnO2 on the photogenerated carrier behaviors of the composites were reflected by PL spectra, photocurrent response and EIS curves. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the PL intensity of CNNT was lower than that of Bulk-CN, and the loading of NPCDs and SnO2 further decreased the PL intensity of catalysts, revealing that the construction of nanotubes and modification of NPCDs and SnO2 are beneficial in inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [43]. The higher photocurrent response of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT proved that it exhibited faster carrier separation rate (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the smaller impedance arc radius of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT also demonstrated that the embellishment of NPCDs and SnO2 could facilitate the transfer of charges (Fig. 5c) [44]. The smaller fitted charge transfer resistance (Rp) derived from the equivalent circuit impedance model also supported this conclusion (Table S3). The photocatalytic activity was further evaluated by the linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) (Fig. 5d). At a current density of 20 mA·cm−2, the overpotential of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT is lower than that of Bulk-CN, CNNT, and NPCDs/CNNT, suggesting that it has higher photocatalytic H2 production activity [45]. In order to evaluate the density of active sites in the photocatalytic reaction, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was tested by cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at different scan rate (Fig. S6). A larger ECSA value means that there are more active sites on the surface of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 5e, the ECSA values of Bulk-CN, CNNT, NPCDs/CNNT, and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT were 2.10, 3.86, 4.95, and 9.58 μF·cm−2. This change trend is consistent with that of the catalyst specific surface area, indicating that the combination of NPCDs and SnO2 is conducive to improving the activity of the catalyst. As expected, the H2 evolution performance of CNNT is better than that of Bulk-CN (Fig. 5f). The properties of hybrids modified by NPCDs or SnO2 were further optimized. The H2 yields of NPCDs/CNNTs and SnO2/CNNTs are 5.5 and 4.4 times higher than that of Bulk-CN, respectively (Fig. 5g, Fig. S7). Amazingly, SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT achieved H2 yield of up to 10.73 mmol·g−1·h−1, which is 12.3 times that of Bulk-CN and superior to many reported catalysts (Table S4). This explicitly confirmed that co-modification of SnO2 and NPCDs synergistically boosted the photocatalytic activity. Fig. S8 depicted the photocatalytic H2 production rate of catalysts loaded with different amounts of SnO2. Strikingly, four photocatalytic cycle tests and 16 h long-term photocatalytic H2 production tests of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT disclosed that it possessed outstanding photocatalytic stability (Fig. 5h, Fig. S9). The HRTEM images, XRD pattern and XPS spectra of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT after photocatalytic testing further verified the stability of the photocatalyst (Fig. S10, Fig. S11). The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT under 420 nm monochromatic light was as high as 18.91% (Fig. 5i). In addition, its AQE matched well with the light absorption spectrum, proving that the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction was induced by the photoexcitation of the catalyst.

To gain more insight into the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT three-phase hybrids, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out. The calculated work functions of CNNT, NPCDs, and SnO2 were 4.50, 4.57, and 9.45 eV, respectively ( Fig. 6a-c), validating their Fermi levels (EF) before contact (Fig. 6d). Once they contact, the electrons at the interface moved from the higher Fermi levels (CNNT and NPCDs) to the lower Fermi levels (SnO2) until their Fermi levels reach equilibrium. In this process, NPCDs located between SnO2 and CNNT worked as conductive carbon bridging channels to facilitate rapid electron transfer. At their interface, the electrons on the CNNT side were depleted, while the electrons on the SnO2 side are accumulated, resulting in a built-in electric field (IEF) along the direction of SnO2 to CNNT and generating band bending [46]. The electron transfer direction was consistent with the XPS results. When illuminated, under the influences of IEF and band bending, the electrons in the CB of SnO2 were more easily recombined with the holes in the VB of CNNT through the carbon bridging channel (NPCDs) to construct an S-scheme heterojunction. The CB, VB and band gap of SnO2 and CNNT were further estimated by DFT calculations (Fig. S12, Table S5), which accorded with the energy band arrangement obtained by experimental tests (Fig. 4d). To further investigate the interface properties of SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT three phases, the average electron density difference (Δρ) at the interface was analyzed (Fig. 6e,f). From the side, the Δρ was negative on the CNNT side and positive on the SnO2 side. However, on the middle NPCDs, the side near to CNNT was positive, while the side adjacent to SnO2 was negative. This clearly indicated that the electron transfer path at the three-phase interface was CNNT−NPCDs−SnO2, and the electrically conductive NPCDs could act as an electron transfer bridge to promote charge separation and transfer. The side view of charge density difference verified this conclusion more intuitively (Fig. 6g). It can be identified from the TEM images that apart from the three-phase interface, there are many two-phase interfaces of SnO2 and CNNT. The contact potential difference (−7.6 eV) between CNNT and SnO2 implies that electrons will transfer from CNNT to SnO2 at the interface (Fig. S13) [47]. The S-scheme heterojunction was also formed between SnO2 and CNNT (Fig. S14). The Δρ and charge density difference at the SnO2/CNNT interface further affirmed the transfer direction of electrons from CNNT to SnO2 (Fig. S15).

To further explore the photocatalytic mechanism, electron spin resonance (ESR) test was performed. As shown in Fig. 7a,b, the signal peak of DMPO−·OH appeared in SnO2 and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT when illuminated, but not in CNNT, suggesting that the VB potential of CNNT was not positive enough to oxidize OH− to·OH (OH−/·OH, 1.99 V vs NHE). The peaks of DMPO−·O2 − were observed in NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT, but not in SnO2, indicating that the CB potential of SnO2 was not negative enough (O2/·O2 −, −0.33 V vs NHE). Surprisingly, after the recombination of SnO2 and NPCDs/CNNT, signal peaks of DMPO−·O2 − and DMPO−·OH were generated in SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction, suggesting that the construction of the heterojunction retained the electrons in the NPCDs/CNNT conduction band and the holes in the SnO2 valence band, which proved the construction of the S-scheme heterojunction. And stronger signal peaks indicated that the construction of heterojunctions promoted charge separation, producing more favorable electrons and holes. Based on the above analysis, a schematic diagram of the photocatalytic reaction mechanism for SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT three-phase complex was proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 7c. When illuminated, the S-scheme SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction was constructed, in which the NPCDs bridge located between SnO2 and CNNT can serve as a charge transport channel. The electrons in the CB of SnO2 will quickly recombine with the holes in the VB of CNNT through the NPCDs carbon bridge, preserving the electrons in the CB of CNNT and the holes in the VB of SnO2, and promoting the separation of electrons and holes. In addition, NPCD, as an electron acceptor, can accept electrons from CB of CNNT for subsequent surface reactions, further facilitating charge separation. Compared with two-phase composites (NPCDs/CNNT and SnO2/CNNT), the SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT three-phase composite presented better photocatalytic H2 yield, thanks to the synergistic effect of S-scheme heterojunction (SnO2 and CNNT) and NPCDs (charge channels and electron acceptors).

In summary, we designed an S-scheme SnO2/NPCDs/CNNT heterojunction photocatalyst with remarkable photocatalytic H2 evolution, achieving H2 yield of 10.73 mmol·g−1·h−1 (12.3 times that of bulk g-C3N4) and excellent cycle stability. This enhancement of photocatalytic activity may stem from the synergistic effect among CNNT nanotubes, SnO2 nanowires, and NPCDs. DFT calculations and experimental results demonstrated that the NPCDs located between CNNT and SnO2 acted as a conductive channel to accelerate the rapid recombination of electrons in the SnO2 conduction band and holes in the CNNT valence band at the interface, which was conducive to retaining more favorable electrons in the CNNT conduction band. The construction of S-scheme heterojunction at the three-phase interface also promoted the separation of electrons and holes, thereby improving the photocatalytic H2 production performance. This study not only synthesized an efficient H2 producing photocatalyst, but also provided a new perspective for promoting charge separation through interface engineering. Simultaneously, the photocatalyst with strong redox property may be applied in other fields.

Yaru Shang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Tianxing Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Gang Chen: Supervision. Ehsan Alborzi: Software. Xue Yong: Software, Supervision. Yu Wang: Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by project s of Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province, China (LH2021B009).
X.Y would like to acknowledge HPC of the University of Sheffield and Leverhulme Trust.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.172667.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchical Bi2WO6/BiFeWO6 n-n heterojunction as an efficient photocatalyst for water splitting under visible light
J. Alloy. Compd.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ghosh, S. Bera, A. Singh, S. Basu, R.N. Basu, Hierarchical Bi2WO6/BiFeWO6 n-n heterojunction as an efficient photocatalyst for water splitting under visible light, J. Alloys Compd., 919 (2022) 165700. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.165700.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Band-structure tenability via the modulation of excitons in semiconductor nanostructures: manifestation in photocatalytic fuel generation
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Ghosh, D. Sarkar, S. Bastia, Y.S. Chaudhary, Band-structure tenability via the modulation of excitons in semiconductor nanostructures: manifestation in photocatalytic fuel generation, Nanoscale, 15 (2023) 10939–10974. https://doi.org/10.1039/d3nr02116e.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure properties and characterization of mullite-type materials Bi2M4O9 and their applications in photocatalysis: a review
J. Environ. Chem. Eng.
